### [Mapping the Lands](https://compete.metactf.com/611/problems#problem1695) 

Connect to our network with ssh \-p 7001 ctf-54df5c6deb99@ssh.dyn.mctf.io. See if you can find a machine on this network running some kind of service\!

---

**Scanning the network:**

We can connect to the challenge with SSH.  
**![][image1]**

I first identified my subnet by running

ip a

![][image2]

Which showed my IP as  192.168.0.215/24

This indicated that the target range is 192.168.0.0/24

So I scanned the target range for a live host

With the IP range, we can use nmap to search for any live host and identify any open ports.  
Through running  
Nmap \-p- 192.168.0.0/24  
![][image3]  
We identified that 192.168.0.72 is up and port 1029 is open, running ms-lsa. Nmap identified ms-lsa, which corresponds to MS-LSARPC.

Accessing this port through curl, 

Curl 192.168.9.72:1029

We get the following returned   
![][image4]

Now we extract the flag MetaCTF{c4rt0gr4ph3r5\_d3light}

**Techniques:**  
IP identification: ip a  
Probing the whole IP range: nmap \-p- 192.168.0.0/24

**What I learned:**  
Looking back at my procedure, I realized that I never explored what MS-LSARPC was. MS-LSARPC is Microsoft Local Security Authority Remote Protocol. This allows the remote client to communicate with the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service on a Windows machine. While it was out of scope, this service is worth noting, as misconfiguration or an unpatched vulnerability could lead to exposing sensitive credential material or possibly privilege escalation. Examples of misconfigurations are SMB signing disabled, null session allowed, and domain controller exposure. Documented vulnerabilities on MS-LSARPC are CVE-2021-36942 and CVE-2022-26925. These vulnerabilities allow an attacker to perform an NTLM Relay Attack by calling a method on the LSARPC interface to coerce the domain controller to perform an outbound NTLM authentication to the attacker.
